findings.description: FolderTheme.getTemplate() constructs File from themeDir+name without any path containment check, allowing directory traversal
findings.reasoning: FolderTheme.getTemplate() constructs a new File from themeDir and user-supplied name without any path traversal validation. Unlike getResourceAsStream() which uses getCanonicalPath().startsWith() check, getTemplate() has no such protection. An attacker can supply a name like "../../etc/passwd" to escape the theme directory. The taint flows from ThemeResource @PathParam "path" → theme.getTemplate(name).

evidence.notes: getTemplate has NO canonical path check. getResourceAsStream DOES have a canonical path check. This inconsistency means template files can be accessed outside the theme directory via path traversal.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a crafted HTTP request with path traversal sequences in the theme resource path parameter, causing FolderTheme.getTemplate() to return a file URL pointing outside the theme directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Keycloak must be configured to use FolderTheme (filesystem-based theme), which is the case when custom themes are deployed to the themes directory

findings.description: ClassLoaderTheme.getResourceAsStream() uses String.startsWith() on URL paths without canonicalization, bypassable via prefix collision or encoded traversal
findings.reasoning: ClassLoaderTheme.getResourceAsStream() checks containment using String.startsWith() on URL path components (not canonical filesystem paths). The rootPath is extracted from the URL using getPath() which returns a raw (non-canonical) path string. The check is: resourceURL.getPath().startsWith(rootPath). Issues: 1) If rootPath does not end with '/', a prefix collision attack is possible (e.g., /theme/keycloak/login/resources vs /theme/keycloak/loginevil/resources). 2) The classloader resolves resourceRoot + path where path can contain ../ sequences - the JVM's classloader may normalize these internally, but the URL.getPath() will reflect the resolved path, allowing traversal to sibling directories. 3) scheme-aware bypass: if path contains 'file:///etc/passwd', the classloader.getResource() may return a file:// URL whose path doesn't start with rootPath, returning null. However the startsWith check on a non-slash-terminated rootPath is the key weakness here.

evidence.notes: The rootPath extracted from rootResourceURL.getPath() may NOT have a trailing slash, enabling a prefix collision: /theme/keycloak/login/resources vs /theme/keycloak/login/resourcesEvil/../other. The startsWith check does not canonicalize before checking, violating the 'canonicalize-then-check' best practice.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker requests a theme resource path containing traversal sequences; ClassLoaderTheme resolves the path via classloader and the resulting URL.getPath() passes the non-slash-terminated startsWith check, allowing access to files outside the resource root

findings.description: FilesPlainTextVaultProvider.obtainSecretInternal() resolves vault secret paths using Path.resolve() without any containment check, allowing path traversal via vault key names
findings.reasoning: FilesPlainTextVaultProvider.obtainSecretInternal() calls vaultPath.resolve(vaultSecretId) where vaultSecretId comes from a VaultKeyResolver applied to the realm and vault key. The KEY_ONLY resolver returns the key as-is. The REALM_UNDERSCORE_KEY resolver only escapes underscores but does NOT sanitize path traversal sequences (../, ..\). The REALM_FILESEPARATOR_KEY resolver uses File.separator which can create subdirectory references. After Path.resolve(), there is NO containment check to verify the resolved path is still under vaultPath. An attacker who can supply a vault key reference containing ../ sequences can escape the vault directory and read arbitrary files accessible to the Keycloak process. Note: Path.resolve(absolutePath) would also override the base path entirely if an absolute path is supplied as vaultSecretId.

evidence.notes: The vault key is user-controlled (comes from application configuration or service calls). With KEY_ONLY resolver, key='../../etc/passwd' would resolve to /vault/../../etc/passwd = /etc/passwd. With REALM_FILESEPARATOR_KEY, using a key like '../etc/passwd' with separator would also escape. No Path.normalize() or startsWith(vaultPath) guard is present.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker configures or provides a vault key reference containing path traversal sequences; FilesPlainTextVaultProvider resolves the key against the vault base path without containment check, reading arbitrary files on the filesystem
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Application must use vault references with key values containing ../ sequences; requires ability to set vault key references in realm/client configuration
exploit_payload.category: Path traversal / arbitrary file read via vault

findings.description: ClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory.getResourceAsStream() uses String.startsWith() on raw URL paths without canonicalization, bypassable via prefix collision
findings.reasoning: ClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory.getResourceAsStream() checks path containment using String.startsWith() on raw URL path strings. The rootPath is obtained from rootResourceURL.getPath() without ensuring it ends with '/'. This enables prefix collision attacks where a resource outside the intended root directory has a path that starts with rootPath as a string prefix but is actually in a different directory (e.g., if rootPath="/theme-resources/resources", an attacker could craft a path resolving to "/theme-resources/resources-other/secret"). The check is not using canonical paths, making it vulnerable to directory traversal via ../ sequences in the user-supplied path parameter.

evidence.code_context:
final String rootPath = rootResourceURL.getPath(); // raw URL path, may not end with '/'
final URL resourceURL = classLoader.getResource(THEME_RESOURCES_RESOURCES + path); // path is user-supplied
if(resourceURL == null || !resourceURL.getPath().startsWith(rootPath)) { // string prefix check, not canonical
evidence.notes: Same vulnerability pattern as ClassLoaderTheme. The check uses String.startsWith on URL path strings without normalization or canonical form. Both FlatClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory (which delegates to this) and ClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory share this weakness.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies a crafted path via HTTP request to ThemeResource; ClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory resolves the path via classloader and the URL path passes the non-slash-terminated startsWith check, exposing files in sibling directories
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Classpath-based theme resource provider in use; resource files present in sibling directories sharing the path prefix

findings.description: FlatClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory.getResourceAsStream() delegates to parent's startsWith check without path canonicalization, inheriting prefix collision bypass
findings.reasoning: FlatClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory.getResourceAsStream() iterates over all classpath entries for THEME_RESOURCES_RESOURCES and delegates to the parent ClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory.getResourceAsStream(path, rootResourceURL) for each. The parent's implementation uses String.startsWith() on raw URL paths without canonicalization (prefix collision vulnerability). This Quarkus-specific implementation inherits that vulnerability. Additionally, by iterating ALL classpath resource roots, the effective resource scope is broader, increasing exposure to sensitive classpath resources like WEB-INF/web.xml or META-INF/MANIFEST.MF if path traversal succeeds.

evidence.notes: The Quarkus-specific flat classpath implementation iterates all classpath resource roots. If any root URL path is a prefix of an attacker-controlled path, the resource will be served. The no-canonicalization startsWith check is inherited from the parent class.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends a path traversal request to QuarkusWelcomeResource; FlatClasspathThemeResourceProviderFactory iterates all classpath roots and the parent's non-canonicalized startsWith check allows access to sensitive classpath resources

findings.description: FolderTheme.getResourceAsStream() uses canonical path check - false positive
findings.reasoning: This is the canonicalize-then-check pattern which is sound. getCanonicalPath() resolves all symlinks and ../ sequences before the containment check. The check also uses + File.separator to avoid prefix collisions (e.g., "resourcesDir/" vs "resourcesDirEvil/"). This is a proper implementation and not vulnerable to path traversal. The CodeQL finding flagging this is a false positive because the path IS properly validated before use.

evidence.notes: Sound canonicalize-then-check pattern. This is correct and safe implementation.

findings.description: SamlMultiTenantResolver in test suite loads classpath resources using realm query param - false positive (test code)
findings.reasoning: This code is located in the testsuite directory (integration-arquillian/test-apps/servlets) and is test/demo code, not production code. The path is constructed as "/" + realm + "-keycloak-saml.xml" where realm comes from a query parameter. While this does look up a classpath resource using user input, this is test servlet code used only in integration test environments, not in production Keycloak. The path is also constrained to files named "{realm}-keycloak-saml.xml" at the classpath root.

evidence.notes: Test suite code only - not a production vulnerability. Located in testsuite directory.

findings.description: FolderTheme.getMessages() constructs File from themeDir without path containment check, allowing directory traversal via locale parameter
findings.reasoning: FolderTheme.getMessages() constructs a File from themeDir using baseBundlename and locale.toString() without any canonical path containment check. Unlike getResourceAsStream() which uses getCanonicalPath() for safety, getMessages() has no such protection. If an attacker can control the locale parameter (e.g., via Accept-Language header or locale query parameter) to inject path traversal sequences like "../../etc/passwd\x00" or manipulate baseBundlename, this could read files outside the theme directory. The locale.toString() call can produce values like "../../etc" if the Locale object was constructed with crafted language/country values. Note: the .properties suffix is appended, limiting file type but not preventing information disclosure of properties-formatted files.

evidence.notes: Consistent with FolderTheme.getTemplate() - both lack the canonicalize-then-check pattern that getResourceAsStream correctly implements. The .properties suffix limits exploitability but doesn't fully prevent directory traversal.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies a crafted locale value (via Accept-Language header or locale query param) containing path traversal sequences; FolderTheme.getMessages() reads a .properties file outside the theme directory
exploit_payload.prerequisites: FolderTheme in use; attacker controls locale parameter; target file must have .properties extension; Keycloak accepts crafted locale values without normalization

findings.description: UndertowWarClassLoader in test utility loads classpath resources - false positive (test infrastructure code)
findings.reasoning: This code is in the testsuite/integration-arquillian/util directory, which is test infrastructure code only. It is not part of the production Keycloak server deployment. The finding flows through SamlMultiTenantResolver (also a test servlet) into this test utility class. Since both source and sink are in test/utility code, this is not an exploitable production vulnerability.

evidence.notes: Test utility code only. Not a production vulnerability.
